build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: The player stocks shelves by dragging items onto display positions and sets prices via a slider or input. Adventurer customers browse, evaluate prices against their visible budget, and buy or leave. Score 0 if no pricing mechanic exists. Score 0.5 at most if items are placed but prices cannot be set or customers ignore prices. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Customers have visible budget indicators and item preferences. Overpriced items are rejected (customer scoffs and leaves). Underpriced items sell quickly but reduce profit. The pricing sweet spot is the core decision. Score 0 if customer behavior is random regardless of price. Score 0.5 at most if budgets exist but are not visible to the player. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Thieves occasionally grab items and run for the door. The player must click to chase or activate pre-placed traps to stop them. Failed theft prevention loses inventory. Score 0 if no theft mechanic exists. Score 0.5 at most if thieves appear but cannot be stopped or have no consequence. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

A dungeon phase lets the player enter a procedural side-scrolling dungeon with simple combat to collect loot for restocking the shop. Better dungeon performance yields better inventory. Score 0 if no dungeon phase exists. Score 0.5 at most if a dungeon exists but loot does not connect to shop inventory. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The run ends in victory (reaching a gold target) or bankruptcy (running out of stock and gold). Both outcomes have distinct result screens. Gold persists between shop and dungeon phases. Score 0 if no win/loss condition exists. Score 0.5 at most if conditions exist but are not clearly communicated. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least 8 distinct item types exist (swords, shields, potions, armor, rings, scrolls, etc.) with different base values and customer appeal. Items have visible quality tiers. Score 0 if fewer than 4 item types exist. Score 0.5 at most if items exist but are functionally identical. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least 4 distinct customer types with different budgets, preferences, and behaviors (bargain hunters, collectors, warriors, mages). Customer mix changes over days. Score 0 if all customers are identical. Score 0.5 at most if types exist but behave the same. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Shop upgrades are purchasable between days: display cases (hold more items), security (traps, alarms), larger floor space, and decorations that attract better customers. At least 4 upgrade types. Score 0 if no upgrades exist. Score 0.5 at most if upgrades exist but fewer than 3 types are available. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

The dungeon phase has procedural generation with at least 3 enemy types and collectible loot of varying quality. Dungeon difficulty increases on later days. Score 0 if the dungeon is static or has no enemies. Score 0.5 at most if procedural generation exists but with no variety. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A results screen shows days survived, total profit, thieves caught, best single sale, and dungeon floors cleared. Score 0 if no results exist. Score 0.5 at most if only basic info is shown. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A styled title screen shows a cozy shop interior with items on display. Score 0 if the game starts directly in gameplay or uses raw default UI. Score 0.5 at most if a title exists but is sparse. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The game flow connects title -> shop day -> dungeon phase -> upgrade screen -> next day without restarting. Score 0 if transitions are missing. Score 0.5 at most if flow exists but is broken. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

During the shop phase, gold balance, item prices, customer budgets, and inventory are clearly visible. During dungeon, health and loot collected are shown. Score 0 if no displays exist. Score 0.5 at most if displays are incomplete. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Readability at 1280x720 is strong: items, customers, prices, shelves, and the shop layout are all clearly distinguishable. Score 0 if elements blend together. Score 0.5 at most if visuals are dominated by raw shapes. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The art direction is coherent across title, shop, dungeon, customers, items, and UI — a consistent fantasy shopkeeper aesthetic with warm palette. Score 0 if mostly default UI or mismatched placeholders dominate. Score 0.5 at most if visuals are sparse or generic. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Items have distinct illustrated sprites, customers have character art, and the shop interior uses real tile/sprite assets — not plain ColorRect fills. Score 0 if items or customers are text labels. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

HUD and UI chrome: price sliders, gold display, customer panels, upgrade shop, and result screens use styled themed widgets — not raw unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0 if primary UI elements are obviously unstyled. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Animation and feedback: customers browse with walking animations, purchases produce coin effects, thieves run with urgency, traps trigger with visual flair, and the dungeon has combat animations. Score 0 if all state changes snap silently. Score 0.5 at most if animation is minimal. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
